items.title: US-Iran trading missile and drone strikes; WTI back above $90, equities shrug
items.idea: The US and Iran exchanged missile and drone strikes overnight, sending WTI crude back above $90 after a brief dip below that level. Yet S&P 500 futures are only -0.2% and Nasdaq -0.33%. The 10-year yield is up just 3bps to 4.51%. Asian equities fell 0.5-1.3% but the US market's resilience to a shooting war is the story — the AI earnings counternarrative is dominating risk sentiment.
items.angle: The AI tech earnings boom is now so dominant that markets are treating an active Middle East war as a minor headwind. This disconnect between bond market (following oil) and equity market (following NVDA) is the trade setup of the moment.
items.tweetA: Oil back above $90 and the bond market is following every tick. Equities? Almost flat. The market has decided AI earnings trump a Middle East war. That's a bet, not a thesis.
items.tweetB: US and Iran trading missile strikes. WTI back above $90. 10yr up 3bps to 4.51%. And the S&P opens -0.2%. The market is treating a shooting war like a speed bump. At some point that complacency has a price.

items.title: S&P breadth negative at 52-week highs — 11 divergences so far this year
items.idea: Yesterday was the 11th time in 2026 that the S&P 500 hit a 52-week high while market breadth was negative. Monster data day today: PCE, GDP, Durable Goods, Jobless Claims all at 8:30am EST.
items.angle: Eleven breadth divergences at new highs in one year is a concentration warning. When fewer stocks are responsible for index gains, the index is more fragile to reversals — especially relevant with NVDA at 9% of the S&P.
items.tweetA: S&P hit a 52-week high yesterday but breadth was negative — the 11th time this year. In the late 1990s this happened constantly. Everyone said it was fine then too.
items.tweetB: A market making new highs with deteriorating breadth is telling you something. 11 divergences in 2026. It is not the same stocks keeping this alive — it is fewer and fewer of them each time.

items.source: David Rosenberg / Early Morning with Dave
items.title: Stagflation: bonds priced in the inflation half, equities haven't priced in the growth risk half
items.idea: Rosenberg's central thesis: the bond market has already priced in the inflation side of stagflation, but equity markets have yet to discount the associated downside growth risks. Fed officials are now openly discussing stagflation scenarios. Consumers buying fewer cars, not filling tanks, carpooling more, and major global airlines seeing demand destruction from trip cancellations.
items.angle: This is the cleanest asymmetric framing of the current setup. Bonds have moved. Equities haven't. One market is wrong. The consumer behavior data — carpooling surging, flights canceled — is the leading indicator equity bulls are ignoring.
items.tweetA: Rosenberg's framing is clean: bonds have priced in the inflation half of stagflation. Equities haven't priced in the growth risk half. One of these markets is wrong.
items.tweetB: Stagflation: the bond market gets it. Equities still in denial. Consumers carpooling, canceling flights, buying fewer cars. What do bond vigilantes know that the S&P doesn't?

items.title: Dallas Fed services -7.7: coffee operator reports 50% fuel surcharges, traffic down 2-6% at 35% of locations
items.idea: From the Dallas Fed May services survey (printed -7.7), a coffee business operator: fuel surcharges of 50% for NJ imports and 26% for Houston; 10%+ cost increases on cups and bags; 35% of cafes seeing 2-6% traffic declines. Their consumer is 'generally amongst the more affluent.' This is the real-economy inflation that official PCE data will catch only with a lag.
items.angle: The gap between survey-based company cost data and official inflation stats is the trade. PCE releases today — if it misses hot, this kind of anecdote explains why. The coffee business description of margin squeeze and demand destruction from the affluent consumer is particularly telling.
items.tweetA: Hidden in the Dallas Fed services survey: a coffee operator reporting 50% fuel surcharges on NJ imports, 10%+ cost increases on cups and bags, and 35% of locations seeing 2-6% traffic declines. This is the inflation the PCE is not showing yet.
items.tweetB: 'Our concern is that input prices are far outpacing official inflation.' — a coffee shop operator in the Dallas Fed May survey. This is why central banks always lag. The data lags the reality by months.

items.title: Rental vacancy rate ticks back DOWN for first time in 4+ years; new rents rising 4 straight months
items.idea: Apartment List May data: new asking rents rose +0.5% MoM for the 4th consecutive month. The vacancy rate is ticking back DOWN for the first time in over four years, falling to 7.2% from 7.3% peak in February — the highest since at least 2017. The disinflation in shelter the Fed has been counting on may be reversing.
items.angle: The shelter disinflation thesis has been a key pillar of the 'inflation is coming down' narrative. Vacancy rates turning lower is the leading indicator that rent inflation could re-accelerate into CPI/PCE prints 3-6 months from now.
items.tweetA: Rental vacancy rate just ticked back DOWN for the first time in over 4 years — from 7.3% to 7.2%. New rents rising for 4 straight months. The disinflation in rents people counted on may be over.
items.tweetB: New asking rents up 0.5% MoM for the 4th straight month. Vacancy rate finally turning lower after peaking at the highest since 2017. The 'shelter inflation will fix itself' trade just got harder.

items.title: Kevin Warsh first press conference June 17th — the only Fed voice that matters right now
items.idea: Multiple Fed officials spoke today (Kashkari, Goolsbee, Cook, Jefferson) — all in wait and see mode with a bias to hike if energy cost pressures spread. Boockvar: I am really only interested in what Kevin Warsh has to say and we will hear him on June 17th at his first press conference. Key questions: balance sheet plans, how to look past energy costs from Hormuz, what framework replaces Powell-era forward guidance.
items.angle: Every word before June 17th is noise. Warsh inherits a balance sheet problem, an oil shock, and a bond market that has been doing the Fed's job for it. His framework on all three will set the tone for H2 2026.
items.tweetA: Kevin Warsh's first press conference is June 17th. Every other Fed official is in wait and see mode. But Warsh will set the tone for the balance sheet, energy pass-through, and the Hormuz calculus. That date matters more than anything before it.
items.tweetB: Boockvar on Fed speak today: just give me Kevin on June 17th. All the other voices are noise. Warsh inherits an oil shock, a fiscal problem, and a bond market that has been the real disciplinarian. His first words will move markets.

items.source: Daily Chartbook via The Bond Beat
items.title: Asset managers: largest net short in Fed Funds futures since early 2023 — the year of four hikes
items.idea: Per Daily Chartbook: asset managers have built their largest net short position in Fed Funds futures since early 2023, a year that saw the Fed hike four times. With Kevin Warsh recently installed as Fed chair and inflation pressures rebuilding from the energy shock, the smart money positioning is explicitly for tightening, not easing.
items.angle: This is one of the most important positioning signals in rates right now. If the Fed doesn't hike, this positioning unwinds in a massive short squeeze. If they do hike, it's already priced. The asymmetry favors careful monitoring of any dovish signal from Warsh.
items.tweetA: Asset managers have their largest net short in Fed Funds futures since early 2023. That was the year the Fed hiked four times. The positioning is already priced for tightening. Either they are right, or this is a massive setup for a squeeze.
items.tweetB: The smart money is now more short Fed Funds futures than at any point since early 2023 — before the last four hikes of the cycle. With Warsh at the helm and oil at $90, the market is betting the next move is UP, not down.

charts.desc: Fed Funds futures net positioning chart — largest short since 2023. Worth saving.

items.source: David Rosenberg / Early Morning with Dave
items.title: Auto and travel demand cracking: consumers carpooling, canceling flights, airlines seeing demand destruction
items.idea: Rosenberg documents multiple demand destruction signals: consumers buying fewer cars, not filling gas tanks to the limit, driving shift toward carpooling, flight cancellations accelerating, and major global airlines explicitly reporting demand destruction. These are classic early-stage stagflation consumer behaviors — substitution and postponement of discretionary purchases.
items.angle: The travel and auto data are the leading edge of what hits corporate earnings in Q3. Airlines guide down first, then autos, then discretionary retail. By the time it shows in CPI, it is already in stock prices.
items.tweetA: Rosenberg on consumer cracking: fewer cars being bought, gas tanks not being filled, carpooling surging, flights being canceled at major global carriers. This is stagflation behavior. And the S&P is at all-time highs.
items.tweetB: Global airlines are seeing demand destruction. Consumers carpooling. New car sales declining. These are not recession indicators yet — but they are the early symptoms of an inflation squeeze on real spending power. Watch Q3 guidance from airlines and autos.

items.source: Deutsche Bank CoTD via The Bond Beat
items.title: DB: Oil-yields correlation strong while war lasts — when it ends, market reverts to fiscal, Fed, and AI capex as drivers
items.idea: DB's Chart of the Day: yields currently driven almost entirely by oil (Iran war correlation). Once the war concludes, DB expects this correlation to weaken materially. What replaces it: Fed pricing (too low), persistent inflation risks pushing term premium higher, larger fiscal deficits, and tech capex keeping downward pressure on savings rates — all structurally bearish for duration.
items.angle: The war is distorting the signal. Once it ends, the underlying bearish duration thesis reasserts. A peace deal would likely cause an initial bond rally that is subsequently faded hard.
items.tweetA: DB makes the key point: right now oil drives yields, yields drive everything else. Once the war ends, the correlation snaps. What replaces oil? Fiscal deficits, tech capex, persistent inflation. All structurally bearish for duration.
items.tweetB: The market structure to understand: USTs follow oil during the Iran war. When the war ends, that correlation breaks. Then yields get driven by fiscal, Fed, and AI capex dynamics. The yield relief trade assumes oil falls AND everything else stays benign. It will not.

charts.desc: DB oil-to-yields correlation chart since start of Iran war. Tight relationship visible.

items.title: Markets are no longer acting as a guardrail on Trump's Iran war decisions
items.idea: Bloomberg analysis: equity markets have ceased to function as a check on the administration's Iran policy. The AI earnings boom is countermanding the oil shock — stocks are up on tech even as oil spikes on war headlines. The bond market still tracks oil, but not enough to force a policy change. Without market pressure, the war can last longer than priced.
items.angle: Geopolitical duration risk is underpriced if markets are not disciplining the decision-makers. The traditional mechanism — financial pain then policy moderation — is short-circuited by the AI rally.
items.tweetA: Bloomberg makes the uncomfortable point: stock markets are no longer functioning as a guardrail on Trump's Iran policy. AI earnings are countermanding the oil shock. Without market pain, the war can continue longer than anyone is pricing.
items.tweetB: Liz Truss was brought down by the bond market in 2022. Trump backed off tariffs when the bond market got yippy. Now? The AI rally is so powerful that markets are giving the administration a hall pass on the war. What changes that calculus?

charts.desc: S&P 500 vs oil price showing divergence since April.

items.source: Wolf Street via The Bond Beat
items.title: Micron $500B to $1T in 48 trading days — Nvidia took 490. History: 50-98% collapses after every prior spike.
items.idea: Micron hit $1T market cap this week, covering the second $500B in just 48 trading days — by far the fastest such move in history (NVDA took 490 days). Micron has a documented history of 50-98% collapses after every spike: after the dot-com bubble it traded below its dot-com high for over 13 years. Most recent pre-AI peak followed by 58% collapse between June 2024 and April 2025. Trump personally hyped the stock at a rally. There are now 12 US companies over $1T market cap.
items.angle: This is the WTF chart of the AI mania. Micron specifically has the most volatile history of any of the megacap names — cyclical memory is a terrible business model for sustained high multiples.
items.tweetA: Micron: $500B to $1T in market cap in 48 trading days. Nvidia took 490. Every prior spike in Micron history was followed by a 50-98% collapse — including a period where the stock traded below its dot-com bubble high for 13 years. But this time it is different.
items.tweetB: Trump said 'Boy, Micron is great' at a rally on Friday. The stock hit $1T this week after racing from $500B in 48 days. That is 10x the speed Nvidia covered the same ground. I am old enough to remember CSCO doing something similar in 1999.

charts.desc: Micron long-term price chart showing all historical spikes and subsequent crashes vs current AI spike.

items.source: Quinn Thompson / Lekker Capital via The Market House
items.title: Quinn Thompson: SPR drain maps exactly to midterm election calendar — to operational minimum
items.idea: The Trump administration approved release of 170 barrels from the SPR — drawing it down to the operational minimum. Quinn Thompson: if you map out the levels of drainage on a weekly basis, it pretty much lasts until the midterms. This is the administration using the strategic reserve as an election tool, keeping oil prices suppressed until November then kicking the problem to whoever is dealing with it post-election.
items.angle: Oil bears counting on SPR releases as an ongoing supply mechanism need to reset their calendar. The SPR cushion expires at the midterms. Post-election oil price risk rises structurally. Dated Brent December 2026 and 2027 strips are the expression Quinn likes.
items.tweetA: Quinn Thompson caught something buried in the headlines: the SPR release schedule maps almost perfectly to the midterm election calendar. The administration drew down to the operational minimum — it runs out of cushion right after midterms. Classic politician move.
items.tweetB: Trump approved releasing 170 barrels to the operational minimum. Quinn Thompson: if you map out the drainage on a weekly basis, it pretty much lasts until the midterms. The oil market suppression is a feature, not a bug — and it expires in November.

items.source: Quinn Thompson / Lekker Capital via The Market House
items.title: AI capex flows moving from semiconductors into power generation — solar, gas, uranium, then silver and platinum
items.idea: Quinn Thompson argues the AI capital cycle has a rotation built in: first compute, then chips, now power generation. He expects capital to flow from semiconductors into power: natural gas, solar, uranium, and then into raw good inputs — silver, platinum. Both parties now accept solar and uranium politically, making them durable investment themes regardless of election outcomes.
items.angle: This is the second-derivative AI trade. Everyone is in semis. Fewer people are in power. Even fewer in silver/platinum. If Thompson's sequencing is right, the power and materials trade has a longer runway than semis.
items.tweetA: Quinn Thompson's rotation thesis: money flows from semiconductors into power generation — natural gas, solar, uranium, and eventually silver and platinum as AI raw input commodities. The AI trade is not one trade, it is a sequence.
items.tweetB: The AI capital cycle: compute then chips then power then raw materials. Quinn Thompson thinks capital is starting to rotate from semis into energy and power generation. Solar and uranium are now accepted by both parties. That bipartisan support matters for duration of the trade.

items.source: Quinn Thompson / Lekker Capital via The Market House
items.title: Housing 1970s parallel: mortgage rates doubled but home prices tripled — expects 30yr rates to hit 8-9%
items.idea: Quinn Thompson's housing thesis: from 1970-1980, mortgage rates doubled from ~6-7% to 12-14% but home prices tripled over the decade. He models this as the analog for today: rising replacement costs (land, labor, concrete, fuel) force nominal prices higher. He expects 30-year mortgage rates to rise to 8-9% over the coming years. Target: inflation + 2-3% on average, inflation + 5-7% in good areas.
items.angle: The framework that rates rising doesn't kill nominal home prices is counterintuitive but historically supported. If correct, the housing bear thesis based on 6.65% mortgages is wrong — it's the right scenario for nominal price appreciation, just terrible for real returns.
items.tweetA: Quinn Thompson's housing thesis: 1970s replay. Mortgage rates doubled from 6% to 14% but home prices tripled over the decade. He is modeling 8-9% 30yr rates ahead. If he is right, locking in a 30yr fixed right now is the trade of the decade.
items.tweetB: The inflation hedge case for housing: every input — land, labor, concrete, fuel — is inflating. Replacement costs force nominal prices higher even as real returns fall. Quinn Thompson: inflation plus 5-7% for good areas. Rates are the perceived risk. Replacement cost is the reality.

items.title: Anthropic Q2 2026: $10.9B revenue; now 35% more than OpenAI — compute cost ratio improving fast
items.idea: Anthropic generated $4.8B in Q1 2026 and is on track for $10.9B in Q2 2026. Per The Information, Anthropic is now generating at least 35% more revenue than OpenAI, reversing their positions from end of 2025. Compute cost ratio: $0.71 per $1 made last quarter, expected $0.56 this quarter. Polymarket: 93% probability Anthropic has higher private valuation than OpenAI this year.
items.angle: $10.9B quarterly revenue annualizes to ~$44B — achieved from basically zero in 2023. The compute cost ratio trajectory is what makes Anthropic's profitability story different from OpenAI. IPO implications for Amazon and Google as major investors are massive.
items.tweetA: Anthropic generated $4.8B in Q1 2026 and is on track for $10.9B in Q2. They are now reportedly generating 35% more revenue than OpenAI. We are watching the fastest enterprise revenue ramp in history — quarterly. Not annually. Quarterly.
items.tweetB: Anthropic spent $0.71 to make $1 last quarter. They expect that to drop to $0.56 this quarter. Path to profitability in 2026. Meanwhile OpenAI is still burning. The compute cost story is the story everyone is missing behind the revenue headline.

items.title: Business AI token spending 13x in 16 months (Ramp data); ByteDance tripling AI capex to $70B in 2026
items.idea: Ramp data: the average business is spending 13x more on AI tokens in May 2026 vs January 2025 — in just 16 months. ByteDance tripling AI capex in 2026 to $70B. Meta raised 2026 AI capex guidance to $125-145B (up from $115-135B). These are accelerating inflections, not plateaus.
items.angle: The 13x spending growth in 16 months is the demand signal that justifies the infrastructure buildout. It is not yet saturation — it is early stage compounding. Implications for grid capacity, cooling, networking, and power generation.
items.tweetA: The average business is spending 13x more on AI tokens in May 2026 vs January 2025 — in just 16 months. ByteDance is tripling AI capex to $70B this year. Meta raised guidance to $125-145B. This is not a bubble. This is demand destruction of every incumbent software category.
items.tweetB: ByteDance: $70B AI capex in 2026. Meta: $125-145B. Average business: 13x more on AI tokens vs 16 months ago. The infrastructure build is accelerating, not decelerating. If you are modeling AI demand as a plateau, you are modeling history, not reality.

items.title: Anthropic compute cost ratio: $0.71 → $0.56 per $1 revenue, trending toward profitability in 2026
items.idea: Anthropic's unit economics are improving quarter-on-quarter. Last quarter: $0.71 spent on compute per $1 revenue. This quarter: expected $0.56. Demis Hassabis (Google DeepMind) is among Anthropic's early investors — notable structural incentive. Polymarket puts probability of Anthropic being worth more than OpenAI at 93%.
items.angle: Unit economics improvement is what separates a venture-backed lab from a sustainable business. The $0.71 to $0.56 trajectory extrapolates to sub-$0.40 within a year at this pace. That is when Anthropic becomes self-funding.
items.tweetA: Anthropic's compute cost ratio: $0.71 per $1 revenue last quarter, expected $0.56 this quarter. That trajectory toward profitability is what separates them from everyone burning VC money with no cost discipline.
items.tweetB: Nobody talks about the unit economics of AI labs. Anthropic spent $0.71 on compute for every $1 they made last quarter. They expect $0.56 this quarter. Watch where this number goes. It is the number that makes Anthropic investable as a public company.

items.title: BTC below $73K on Iran escalation + $2.6B ETF outflows in 8 days; ETH down 33% YTD below $2,000
items.idea: Bitcoin at $73,335 — a 6-week low, down 3.3% in 24hrs. Two simultaneous drivers: Iran war escalation and the worst BTC ETF outflow day of 2026 (Tuesday: -$733.4M). Two-day outflow total: $1B+. Eight consecutive days of outflows have erased $2.6B+ from Bitcoin ETFs since May 15. Ethereum broke below $2,000 for the first time since April, now down 33% YTD. HYPE — the year's standout at +123% YTD — is down 9.4% today.
items.angle: The $2.6B in 8 days is the institutional ETF bid reversing. If the macro environment (Iran war + oil + potential rate hikes) persists, this outflow trend can continue. ETH at -33% YTD with BTC at -10% is dramatic underperformance that was not on most people's 2026 bingo card.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin ETFs have bled $2.6B over 8 straight days of outflows. ETH is down 33% YTD and just broke below $2,000 for the first time since April. If you thought the institutional adoption wave was a one-way ticket — this is what it looks like when institutions also sell.
items.tweetB: BTC at $73K. ETH down 33% YTD. $733M in BTC ETF outflows in a single day — worst since January. Iran escalation plus end of ETF honeymoon equals two reasons to sell. The 6-week low is a technical and psychological level that matters.

items.title: Bitcoin options max pain at $75K — market is $1,700 below heading into today's $6.25B expiry
items.idea: Today's options expiry: $6.25B notional with max pain at $75,000. Spot at $73,335 — $1,700 below max pain. Dealers have structural incentives to see price drift toward $75K into settlement. The outcome either validates the short squeeze setup or confirms bearish flow has overwhelmed mechanical hedging.
items.angle: Max pain dynamics are real in crypto options because the market is smaller and dealer positioning matters more than in equities. A $1,700 gap to max pain is large but not unprecedented.
items.tweetA: Today's options expiry: max pain at $75K. Spot at $73.3K. That $1,700 gap matters for the options market settlement. Either spot rips $1,700 into expiry — or a lot of calls expire worthless and the sellers book the premium. Watch how this resolves.
items.tweetB: BTC max pain today at $75K with spot at $73.3K — $1,700 below. Dealers have real interest in getting Bitcoin toward that level into settlement. $6.25B notional expiring. This could be a short-term catalyst. Could also be a coin flip in a bear market.

items.title: Jefferies: crypto IPO wave could create $1T public market; Kraken, Blockchain.com, Gemini in pipeline
items.idea: Jefferies, after hosting its first Digital Assets Investor Conference with 35 company execs and 150 institutional investors, projects the crypto sector could become a $1T public market within 5 years. Key observation: institutional investors have shifted from BTC price speculation to blockchain infrastructure integration. IPO pipeline: Kraken/Payward ($20B valuation raise, confidential S-1), Blockchain.com (S-1 filed last week), Gemini.
items.angle: The irony: crypto companies are going public right when crypto trading volumes are down 75% YTD. This is the institutional finance co-opting narrative — infrastructure value capture vs speculation narrative.
items.tweetA: Jefferies surveyed institutional investors at their first crypto conference: they are no longer asking about BTC price. They are asking about tokenized money market funds, private credit settlement, and clearing infrastructure. The institutional question shifted while retail was not watching.
items.tweetB: Crypto IPO pipeline per Jefferies: Kraken at $20B, Blockchain.com S-1 filed, Gemini coming. They project a $1T public crypto market within 5 years. The irony: crypto companies are going public right when crypto trading volumes are down 75% YTD.

items.title: Tether/Cantor Fitzgerald PAC helps Paxton oust Cornyn in Texas GOP primary — crypto going explicitly Republican
items.idea: Crypto-focused PACs spent $9M+ in Texas primaries. Fellowship PAC (Tether and Cantor Fitzgerald) spent $500K backing Ken Paxton's challenge to incumbent GOP Sen. John Cornyn. Paxton is more aggressive on crypto deregulation than Cornyn. The Winklevoss twins launched GOP-only groups. Structure: FairShake plays bipartisan offense, new PACs run exclusively Republican — two-track political coverage.
items.angle: This is a qualitative shift in how crypto money buys political outcomes. The Tether/Cantor/Winklevoss axis is now explicitly in the Republican party-building business. Long-term: crypto deregulation as a permanent Republican platform position.
items.tweetA: Tether and Cantor Fitzgerald's Fellowship PAC just helped Ken Paxton oust John Cornyn in a Texas GOP Senate primary. Paxton is more aggressive on crypto deregulation. This is crypto buying specific regulatory outcomes — not just broad industry access. The lobbying game just escalated.
items.tweetB: FairShake plays bipartisan. New crypto PACs play exclusively Republican. Result: two parallel political layers, one in each party. The Winklevoss twins and Tether/Cantor are now running their own GOP-only operations. $9M spent in Texas alone this cycle.

items.title: Google engineer used internal Year in Search data to bet $3.8M on Polymarket — $1.2M profit; 2nd federal insider trading case
items.idea: Federal prosecutors charged Michele Spagnuolo (36, Italian, Switzerland-based) with using confidential Google Year in Search data to bet on Polymarket most-searched person of 2025. Operated as 'AlphaRaccoon,' transferred $3.8M USDC, made ~$1.2M profit. Key bet: $381.12 stake on singer d4vd at 0.2% implied probability netted ~$200K. Also bet $613K no against Pope Leo. Released on $2.25M bond. CFTC filed parallel civil case. Second Polymarket federal insider trading case — first was a Special Forces soldier who bet on the Maduro raid he participated in.
items.angle: Two federal insider trading cases against Polymarket users in one year. The CFTC is treating prediction markets as regulated financial instruments. Polymarket is moving toward mandatory KYC. The era of pseudonymous prediction markets is ending.
items.tweetA: Google engineer used internal Year in Search data to bet $3.8M on Polymarket. Made $200K from a $381 bet at 0.2% odds. Total profit $1.2M. Second federal Polymarket insider trading case this year. The legal maturation of prediction markets is accelerating fast.
items.tweetB: The second Polymarket insider trading case: a Google engineer used confidential search trend data to predict who the most-searched person of 2025 would be. This is why Polymarket is moving toward mandatory KYC. The era of anonymous prediction market edge is ending.

items.title: OpenZeppelin co-founder: ALL of DeFi is unsafe because AI coding agents are superhuman at finding vulnerabilities
items.idea: Manuel Araoz, former CTO and co-founder of OpenZeppelin (DeFi's foundational security firm), says he now considers all of DeFi unsafe and has advised friends and family to exit blue-chip protocols including Aave, MakerDAO, and Compound. His thesis: coding agents are superhuman at finding vulnerabilities, and smart contract security is too asymmetric — defenders need to fix every bug while attackers need just one exploit to steal funds. Over $1.1B lost to DeFi hacks in the past 12 months including a $292M Kelp DAO exploit (Lazarus Group).
items.angle: This is a threat model update, not FUD. When the person who built the dominant smart contract audit firm exits DeFi blue chips because of AI, the security calculus for the whole space has changed. The asymmetry argument is structurally correct.
items.tweetA: OpenZeppelin co-founder Manuel Araoz: coding agents are now superhuman at finding vulnerabilities, and smart contract security is too asymmetric — defenders need to fix every bug while attackers need just one exploit. He exited Aave, MakerDAO, Compound. $1.1B stolen in DeFi last 12 months.
items.tweetB: The argument against DeFi in the AI era: defenders must patch every vulnerability, attackers only need one. AI models are now better at finding exploits than humans are at fixing them. When the former OpenZeppelin CTO exits blue-chips, that is an informed threat model update.

items.title: Stablecoin mainstream moment: Cash App USDC to 60M users; SoFi first US national bank stablecoin on public blockchain
items.idea: Multiple simultaneous stablecoin adoption events: Block Cash App rolled out USDC to ~60M users (Jack Dorsey had said in March he didn't want to but reversed). SoFi launched SoFiUSD on Ethereum and Solana — first US national bank to offer a stablecoin directly to retail on a public blockchain (15M members). Mastercard secured NY BitLicense. DTCC connecting tokenized asset platform to Stellar. Total stablecoin market cap hit record $322B. Banca Sella became first Italian bank to receive crypto services authorization under MiCA.
items.angle: The dam broke on multiple fronts simultaneously. The US and EU settlement infrastructure is being rebuilt on public blockchains in real time. Tokenized money markets, cross-border payments, and settlement are all converging.
items.tweetA: Jack Dorsey said in March: I do not like that we are going to support stablecoins. This week Cash App started rolling out USDC to all 60M users. Total stablecoin market cap just hit a record $322B. SoFi became the first US national bank to put a stablecoin in a consumer banking app. The dam broke.
items.tweetB: SoFi: first US national bank stablecoin to retail on a public blockchain. Cash App: USDC to 60M users. Mastercard: NY BitLicense secured. DTCC connecting to Stellar. This is not incremental. This is the settlement layer of traditional finance being rebuilt in public.

items.title: Humanoid robots 2025: 16,000 units sold, zero economic output — elder care requires 30,000x improvement in failure rate
items.idea: 13,000-16,000 humanoid robots were sold globally in 2025. None produced any economic output. Of ~14,000 made in China: 9,000 went to universities/research, 3,000 to data collection, 2,000 to shows and exhibitions. Current best-in-class failure rate: miss 1 in 10 tasks under bounded conditions; drop to 40-60% success when task instructions are rephrased. For elder care viability: need 99.9996% per-task success — approximately 30,000x lower than current state-of-the-art. For construction: 10,000x. For domestic use: 200x.
items.angle: The hype-to-reality gap in humanoid robots is one of the most spectacular in tech history. The demos are impressive. The deployment-ready timeline is a decade away, minimum.
items.tweetA: 13,000-16,000 humanoid robots were sold globally in 2025. Zero produced any economic output. 9,000 went to universities, 3,000 to data collection, 2,000 to shows. The humanoid robot revolution is currently an elaborate PR exercise.
items.tweetB: For elder care robots to be viable: need 99.9996% per-task success. Current best is 90%. That is a 30,000x improvement needed. For construction: 10,000x. For domestic work: 200x. The demos are impressive. The math on deployment-ready is brutal.

items.title: China dominates humanoid supply chain at 63%; Tesla Optimus $46K with Chinese parts vs $131K without; China 20x more output in 2025
items.idea: China made over 20x more humanoid robots than the US in 2025. 63% of the global humanoid supply chain is in China (rare earth magnets, batteries, LiDAR). Tesla Optimus Gen 2 costs approximately $46,000 with Chinese suppliers and approximately $131,000 without — a 2.8x cost penalty for supply chain decoupling. Tim Cook: in the US you could have a meeting of tooling engineers and not fill the room; in China you could fill multiple football fields.
items.angle: The supply chain reality for US humanoid robot ambitions is brutal: 63% Chinese component dependence, 2.8x cost disadvantage at decoupling, and a manufacturing labor gap that has not closed.
items.tweetA: Tesla Optimus Gen 2: $46K with Chinese suppliers. $131K without. 63% of the global humanoid supply chain is in China. If you are building a US humanoid robot company, you are building on Chinese components — and tariff policy will eventually find you.
items.tweetB: China made 20x more humanoid robots than the US in 2025. They dominate the supply chain. Tim Cook famously said: in the US you could have a meeting of tooling engineers and not fill the room. In China you could fill multiple football fields. That gap has not closed.

items.title: Industrial robots drove 16% of US productivity growth 1993-2007 — same as railroads in 19th century; receive zero media coverage
items.idea: Over 5 million industrial robots installed worldwide, adding trillions to the economy annually. Robots handle 90%+ of weld jobs and 85%+ of paint jobs at BMW and Mercedes. Amazon has 1M+ automated mobile robots. TSMC's automation reduced staff by 95%. From 1993-2007, robotics drove 16% of overall productivity growth — the same proportion railroads contributed in the 19th century. Yet industrial robots receive almost no media attention while humanoid demos reach billions of social media impressions.
items.angle: The real automation revolution is invisible because it is not anthropomorphic. This is a cultural observation with investment implications: the businesses built on industrial automation (FANUC, ABB, Keyence, Rockwell) have been compounding quietly for decades with none of the hype premium.
items.tweetA: Industrial robots drove 16% of overall US productivity growth from 1993 to 2007 — roughly the same contribution railroads made in the 19th century. 5 million installed worldwide. And they get zero media coverage while humanoid demos get billions of impressions without shipping a single commercial product.
items.tweetB: The most economically important robot category — industrial robots — is invisible to media. Amazon has over 1 million automated mobile robots. TSMC automated away 95% of its workforce. This happened silently. The humanoid that folded laundry on social media got 10 million views.

items.title: AppLovin Q1 2026: $1.84B revenue +59% YoY, 85% EBITDA margin, net margin 65% — stock +10%
items.idea: AppLovin Q1 2026: Revenue $1.84B (+59% YoY from $1.16B), net income $1.21B (+109% YoY), net margin ~65%, adjusted EBITDA $1.56B at ~85% margin. EPS $3.56 vs $3.44 consensus. Cash $2.76B up from $0.54B a year ago. Buybacks: $1B in Q1. Q2 2026 guidance: revenue $1.915-1.945B at 84-85% EBITDA margins. Management: AI advertising platform just getting started.
items.angle: The 85% EBITDA margin at $1.84B quarterly revenue is rare. AppLovin has successfully transitioned the narrative from ad cyclical to AI-enhanced mobile ad infrastructure platform. The buyback discipline while growing at 59% is the capital allocation proof point.
items.tweetA: AppLovin Q1 2026: $1.84B revenue plus 59% YoY, net margin 65%, EBITDA margin 85%. They made more in Q1 than many companies make in a year and they are growing at 59%. At 85% EBITDA margins, AXON 2.0 is one of the most efficient businesses ever built.
items.tweetB: AppLovin stock up 10% today. $1.84B revenue, 85% EBITDA margins, $1B in buybacks in a single quarter. Management says they are just getting started. They are guiding Q2 to $1.92B. Still no signs of deceleration. $APP is what AI-enhanced software looks like at scale.

items.title: Zscaler -31.5% — largest single-day drop ever: FY27 ARR guide cut to 16-17% vs 23-25% expected
items.idea: Zscaler fiscal Q3 2026: revenue ~$850M (+25% YoY), adjusted EPS ~$1.08 (beat vs ~$0.90 consensus). Yet the stock fell 31.5% — the largest single-day drop in company history. Cause: management guided FY27 ARR growth at 16-17%, vs sell-side consensus anchored at low-to-mid 20s. Additionally: two sales leaders recently departed, go-to-market reorg underway. Evercore ISI downgraded to in-line. Prior beat-and-raise pattern broken.
items.angle: The SaaS multiple compression template: once a company breaks from hyper-growth to solid growth, the repricing is immediate and violent. The guidance was only 7-9 percentage points below expectation. The stock moved 31%.
items.tweetA: Zscaler dropped 31.5% today — the largest single-day decline in company history. The crime: guiding FY27 ARR growth at 16-17% instead of the 23-25% the market expected. Sales leadership turnover. Go-to-market reorg. When growth decelerates in SaaS, the multiple compression is violent.
items.tweetB: Zscaler: beats Q3 on revenue and earnings. Then guides FY27 ARR at 16-17%. Stock falls 31.5%. This is the SaaS template in 2026: a single guidance miss from hyper-growth to solid growth equals minus 30% instantly. The market pays up for compounding certainty. Remove the certainty, pay the price.

items.title: Black-Scholes won a Nobel Prize. LTCM blew up using it 12 months later. The assumptions are the entire risk profile.
items.idea: The Black-Scholes model assumes: constant volatility, no jumps, frictionless markets, continuous hedging, lognormal returns. Each assumption marks a place where the real market separates from the math. The volatility smile has existed since the 1987 crash — the market's permanent refutation of the constant-vol assumption. Trading floors: the wrong number in the wrong formula to get the right price. Robert Merton won the 1997 Nobel Prize for option pricing. LTCM, which he sat on the board of, lost $4.6B the following year.
items.angle: This is the foundational parable of quantitative finance: the model that wins a Nobel Prize is also the model that fails first in a crisis, precisely because the crisis violates the assumptions used to build the model.
items.tweetA: Robert Merton won the 1997 Nobel Prize for the Black-Scholes option pricing model. The fund he advised lost $4.6 billion the following year. The assumptions that make the math work — constant volatility, no jumps, continuous hedging — are exactly the assumptions that fail in a crisis.
items.tweetB: The volatility smile has existed since the 1987 crash and has never gone away. It is the market saying: your model assumes tails are impossible; we disagree. Black-Scholes is, in the words of trading floors, the wrong number in the wrong formula to get the right price. It works until it does not.

items.title: CFTC vacated its own 2025 Gemini consent order — found original case should never have been filed
items.idea: The CFTC moved to vacate its own 2025 consent order against Gemini, concluding after comprehensive review that the original complaint should never have been filed under current enforcement standards. The agency found: the case relied on a witness of known limited credibility; staff pursued Gemini as a fraud victim rather than a perpetrator; internal staff improperly used CFTC regulatory authority to create settlement leverage. A regulator admitting its own enforcement action should never have been brought is almost unprecedented.
items.angle: Combined with SEC pivot to rulemaking from enforcement, the regulatory reset for crypto is progressing faster than most realize. A regulator admitting enforcement overreach in public is the Gensler era being unwound in real time.
items.tweetA: The CFTC just vacated its own 2025 consent order against Gemini — concluded after review that the original complaint should never have been filed. Staff had used regulatory authority as settlement leverage. The Gensler era enforcement machine is being reversed in public.
items.tweetB: The CFTC not only dropped the case against Gemini — they said the original enforcement action should never have been brought. The agency found its own staff had improperly used regulatory power to create settlement leverage. This is a remarkable admission. And it is the direction of travel now.

items.title: Barclays 71st Equity Gilt Study: Embodied AI + humanoid robotics could drive a new macro regime for asset prices
items.idea: Barclays 71st annual Equity Gilt Study examines how the rise of embodied AI and humanoid robotics could drive a new macro regime — reshaping productivity, labor markets, and asset prices as automation moves into the physical economy. First major annual study to treat physical-world AI as a macro variable. Also documents: US equities delivered strong returns in 2025 despite tariff wobble; 10y UST yields hit 4.0% in April and recovered to 4.6%; Fed cut 3x in Q4 2025.
items.angle: When Barclays dedicates their flagship annual macro study to physical AI as a macro regime question, it marks the mainstreaming of the robotics-as-macro thesis. The question for asset allocators: what portfolio structure survives a world where physical automation inflects?
items.tweetA: Barclays' 71st annual Equity Gilt Study is out. This year's theme: how embodied AI and humanoid robotics could reshape the macro regime — productivity, labor markets, and asset prices. They are treating physical-world automation as a macro variable now, not just a sector theme.
items.tweetB: The central question for the next macro regime per Barclays: what happens to labor markets, capital allocation, and asset prices when AI enters the physical economy? The Equity Gilt Study is the right place for that question. The answer is not obvious but the timing of onset matters more than the answer.
